task: I’m trying to turn one brand concept into a few different social posts without making each version feel like a full redesign. What’s the simplest way to set that up so I can move fast and keep the visuals consistent?

turns.agent_response:
The simplest setup is to build **one master system** and vary only **3 things**:

### 1) Keep one fixed base
Lock these across every version:
- **Layout grid**
- **Type hierarchy**
- **Logo placement**
- **Core color palette**
- **One recurring image treatment**  
  (same crop logic, filter, grain, border, or motion style)

That’s what keeps it from feeling like a new design every time.

### 2) Make each post a controlled variation
Only change:
- **Headline**
- **Hero image / clip**
- **One accent color or graphic element**
- **One supporting line or CTA**

So each post feels like a new beat, not a new system.

### 3) Build a “module” approach
Think in reusable parts:
- **Open**
- **Main message**
- **Detail shot / proof point**
- **Close / logo lockup**

Then you can swap the content inside the modules without changing the structure.

---

## Fastest practical setup
If you want to move quickly, make:
- **1 master template**
- **3–5 content variants**
- **1 rules page** that says what never changes

### Your rules page should say:
- what stays fixed
- what can change
- what colors are allowed
- what crop/motion style to use
- where the logo always lands

---

## Easiest way to keep consistency
Use a single visual “anchor” in every post:
- same background treatment
- same type style
- same framing
- same motion rhythm
- same end card

Even if the content changes, that anchor makes the set feel connected.

If you want, I can turn this into a **simple post system** for:
- static
- carousel
- short video
- story/reel

so you can use the same concept across formats without redesigning each one.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, direction’s right. But I want this tighter — give me an actual **template system**, not just principles.

Like:
- what the master layout is
- what stays locked every time
- what I should swap per post
- how many variations I can push before it starts looking recycled

And if you can, keep it in a way I could hand to a designer or build myself in Figma without it turning into a mess.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — set it up as a **template system with one master frame and a few controlled variables**.

## 1) Master layout
Build **one base composition** for every post:

**Structure**
- **Top or center:** headline zone
- **Middle:** hero image / clip / product
- **Bottom:** support line / CTA / logo lockup

**Best practice**
- Use a **12-column grid** or a simple **2-zone / 3-zone layout**
- Keep **logo in the same corner or end position every time**
- Keep the **type hierarchy identical** across all posts

Think of it like:
- **Frame**
- **Message**
- **Proof**
- **Exit**

That’s your reusable skeleton.

---

## 2) What stays locked
These should not change unless you’re making a new system:

- **Grid / spacing system**
- **Typography pairings**
- **Logo placement**
- **Core color palette**
- **Image treatment**  
  (crop style, grain, filter, border, shadow, motion language)
- **CTA style**
- **Corner radius / shapes / line weight**
- **Overall composition logic**

If these stay fixed, the posts will feel related even when the content changes.

---

## 3) What you swap per post
Only change the parts that carry the message:

- **Headline**
- **Supporting copy**
- **Hero image or clip**
- **Accent color**
- **One graphic motif or icon**
- **CTA line**
- **Optional proof point**  
  (quote, stat, feature, benefit)

Rule of thumb:  
**swap content, not structure.**

---

## 4) How many variations before it feels recycled
A good range is:

- **3–5 variations** if the audience sees them close together
- **6–8 variations** if the system has strong image diversity
- **2–3 variations** if the posts are very minimal or text-led

If everything uses the same crop, same headline length, same color accent, and same end frame, it’ll start to feel repetitive fast.

### To avoid that:
Vary only **one or two dimensions at a time**:
- image type
- copy length
- accent color
- layout emphasis
- crop scale

So each post feels like a new beat, not a duplicate.

---

## 5) The simplest Figma setup
Make it as a **component system**:

### Base component
- master frame
- locked grid
- text styles
- logo lockup
- background treatment

### Swap layers
- headline text
- subcopy
- image placeholder
- accent shape
- CTA

### Variants
Create 3–5 named variants like:
- **Promo**
- **Feature**
- **Quote**
- **Announcement**
- **Culture / behind-the-scenes**

That way the designer can duplicate and swap content without rebuilding anything.

---

## 6) The rule sheet
Give the designer a one-page rule list:

**Locked**
- grid
- type styles
- logo position
- palette
- treatment

**Flexible**
- headline
- image
- CTA
- accent color
- one graphic element

**Limits**
- max headline length
- max 2 accent colors per batch
- no new fonts
- no new layout changes
- no new logo placement

---

## 7) Practical recommendation
If you want the fastest, cleanest setup:

- Build **1 master template**
- Create **3 core variants**
- Use **1 shared visual treatment**
- Change only **copy + image + one accent**

That’s usually enough to make the set feel cohesive without looking mass-produced.

If you want, I can turn this into a **Figma-ready template spec** with:
- frame sizes
- layer structure
- component names
- locked vs editable fields

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the structure I wanted. Clean enough to hand off.

One thing though — I’d push the variation cap a little more specific:
- **3 posts** = safest, feels intentional
- **4–5 posts** = still fine if the image/content changes enough
- **6+** = only if you’re rotating crop, subject, and copy rhythm, otherwise it starts looking like you cloned it

And for Figma, I’d want it broken down like this:

**Master frame**
- fixed grid
- fixed type scale
- fixed logo position
- fixed background treatment
- fixed CTA placement

**Editable slots**
- headline
- subcopy
- hero image / video still
- accent color
- proof point / stat / quote

**Variant logic**
- Promo
- Feature
- Quote
- Announcement
- BTS / culture

That’s enough. No need to overbuild it.

Also — the part that matters most is the **anchor**. Same end card, same type feel, same spacing rhythm. If that stays locked, you can get away with a lot more change in the middle without it feeling like a new campaign every time.
